expectations.text: Recommends Advanced JSON Cards (or the two-tier hybrid) as the primary format for resident structured facts
expectations.evidence: 'Storage format: Advanced JSON Cards (Tier 1) + raw conversation storage (Tier 2).' The answer leads with Advanced JSON Cards as the primary resident format.

expectations.text: Explains the two-tier architecture: structured facts always in context (Tier 1) plus raw conversation retrieval on demand (Tier 2)
expectations.evidence: 'Tier 1 (always in context, ~500 tokens) gives the agent overview for every response. Tier 2 (retrieved on demand) provides details when needed.' Injection pattern also described.

expectations.text: Covers at least 3 formats on the spectrum with their tradeoffs
expectations.evidence: Answer only discusses Advanced JSON Cards and raw conversation storage. It does not enumerate the spectrum (Simple Notes → Enhanced Notes → JSON Cards → Advanced JSON Cards → Code) or compare multiple formats.

expectations.text: Mentions the backstory field or contextual disambiguation capability of Advanced JSON Cards
expectations.evidence: 'Advanced JSON Cards add backstory, person, relationship, and timestamp fields that enable disambiguation... The backstory field records why a fact was stored.' Multiple backstory examples shown.

expectations.text: Does NOT just say 'use a database' without addressing the format/structure question specific to agent memory
expectations.evidence: Answer provides a detailed JSON Card schema with specific fields (backstory, person, preferences, relationships, context) and explains the injection pattern — not a generic database recommendation.

expectations.text: Recommends Advanced JSON Cards (or the two-tier hybrid) as the primary format for resident structured facts
expectations.evidence: Baseline recommends a generic typed memory schema with PostgreSQL+pgvector. No mention of Advanced JSON Cards or the two-tier hybrid architecture.

expectations.text: Explains the two-tier architecture: structured facts always in context (Tier 1) plus raw conversation retrieval on demand (Tier 2)
expectations.evidence: Baseline uses top-k semantic retrieval at query time — not a two-tier architecture where structured facts are always in context and raw conversations are retrieved on demand.

expectations.text: Covers at least 3 formats on the spectrum with their tradeoffs
expectations.evidence: Baseline presents only one schema (typed memory entries with id/type/content/source/confidence/timestamps/tags). No spectrum of formats is discussed.

expectations.text: Mentions the backstory field or contextual disambiguation capability of Advanced JSON Cards
expectations.evidence: Baseline has no backstory field or equivalent contextual disambiguation mechanism. It uses confidence scores and tags but not per-fact provenance context.

expectations.text: Does NOT just say 'use a database' without addressing the format/structure question specific to agent memory
expectations.evidence: Baseline is primarily a database architecture answer (PostgreSQL+JSONB+pgvector) without addressing agent-specific memory format concerns like what to keep always in context vs. retrieve on demand.

expectations.text: Identifies timestamp-based priority as a resolution strategy (most recent explicit statement wins)
expectations.evidence: 'Timestamp-based priority: Austin wins (1 week ago > 3 months ago). Update the Tier 1 JSON Card to reflect Austin as the current address.'

expectations.text: Mentions contextual disambiguation — both addresses may be correct in different contexts (e.g., work vs personal)
expectations.evidence: 'If the two addresses served different contexts (e.g., "work address" vs. "home address"), the backstory field would disambiguate and both would be retained as non-conflicting facts.'

expectations.text: References the backstory field or similar mechanism for recording context of each stored fact
expectations.evidence: Answer shows a JSON example with backstory fields: 'User stated "I moved to Austin" in session 12' and 'Mentioned as home address in session 3, superseded by Austin move'.

expectations.text: Describes or implies a pipeline approach (compare new fact against existing, decide ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP)
expectations.evidence: 'The Mem0 pattern applied: Extract candidate fact → Compare against existing → Decide: UPDATE (same fact type — home address — with new value)'

expectations.text: Does NOT just say 'delete the older one' without considering that both may be valid in different contexts
expectations.evidence: 'Don't delete San Francisco — demote it. The backstory field enables contextual disambiguation.' Explicitly rejects simple deletion.

expectations.text: Identifies timestamp-based priority as a resolution strategy (most recent explicit statement wins)
expectations.evidence: 'Default rule: More recent information wins. If the user said "Austin" last week and "San Francisco" 3 months ago, assume they moved. Update the memory entry for "location" to "Austin".'

expectations.text: Mentions contextual disambiguation — both addresses may be correct in different contexts (e.g., work vs personal)
expectations.evidence: 'Ambiguity detection: Before overwriting, check if the contradiction might be contextual rather than a correction: "I live in SF" vs "I'm in Austin this week" — one is permanent, one is temporary.'

expectations.text: References the backstory field or similar mechanism for recording context of each stored fact
expectations.evidence: Baseline uses source field and superseded_by/supersedes links but has no backstory field or equivalent mechanism for recording the context/provenance of why a fact was stored.

expectations.text: Describes or implies a pipeline approach (compare new fact against existing, decide ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP)
expectations.evidence: 'When writing a new memory that contradicts an existing one (same type + same semantic field), don't silently overwrite. Mark the old entry as status: superseded.' Implies a compare-then-decide pipeline.

expectations.text: Does NOT just say 'delete the older one' without considering that both may be valid in different contexts
expectations.evidence: 'Never silently delete: Keep the history. The old memory might be useful context ("user relocated from SF to Austin in March 2024").' Also checks for contextual vs. correction ambiguity.

expectations.text: Presents a multi-level evaluation framework (at least 3 levels)
expectations.evidence: 'Use the Three-Level Evaluation framework' with Level 1, Level 2, and Level 3 explicitly defined.

expectations.text: Level 1 covers basic single-session recall
expectations.evidence: 'Level 1 — Basic Recall (storage and retrieval work): Store a fact in session 1. Query it in session 2. Does the agent return the correct value?'

expectations.text: Level 2 covers multi-session retrieval and/or disambiguation across sessions
expectations.evidence: 'Level 2 — Multi-Session Retrieval (aggregation and disambiguation): Store related facts across multiple sessions. Query something that requires reconciling them.'

expectations.text: Level 3 covers proactive service — anticipating needs the user hasn't expressed
expectations.evidence: 'Level 3 — Proactive Service (synthesis and anticipation): The agent connects facts from different sessions to surface information the user hasn't asked for.'

expectations.text: Notes that most systems only reach Level 1 and that higher levels require deliberate design
expectations.evidence: 'Most systems plateau at Level 1. If you're only testing basic recall, you have no visibility into whether your memory system actually provides value.'

expectations.text: Provides at least one concrete test scenario example (not just abstract descriptions)
expectations.evidence: Multiple concrete examples: Level 1: 'My account number is 12345' → next session: 'What's my account number?'; Level 3: passport expiry + Tokyo trip → proactive renewal reminder.

expectations.text: Presents a multi-level evaluation framework (at least 3 levels)
expectations.evidence: Baseline presents 7 distinct metrics (recall, precision, freshness, contradiction, behavioral impact, write accuracy, latency) but not a leveled framework. These are parallel dimensions, not progressive levels.

expectations.text: Level 1 covers basic single-session recall
expectations.evidence: 'Recall (retrieval accuracy): When a memory is relevant to the current conversation, does the system actually retrieve it?' This covers basic recall, though not framed as a level.

expectations.text: Level 2 covers multi-session retrieval and/or disambiguation across sessions
expectations.evidence: Baseline mentions 'Freshness correctness' (temporal updates) and 'Contradiction handling' but does not address multi-session retrieval or disambiguation across sessions as a distinct capability level.

expectations.text: Level 3 covers proactive service — anticipating needs the user hasn't expressed
expectations.evidence: Baseline has no concept of proactive service or anticipating unexpressed needs. The 'Behavioral impact' metric measures whether memory improves responses, not whether the agent proactively surfaces connections.

expectations.text: Notes that most systems only reach Level 1 and that higher levels require deliberate design
expectations.evidence: Baseline does not note that most systems plateau at basic recall or that higher-level capabilities require deliberate architectural design.

expectations.text: Provides at least one concrete test scenario example (not just abstract descriptions)
expectations.evidence: 'Build a synthetic test suite with scripted multi-session conversations. Session 1 establishes facts. Session 2 (days later) tests retrieval. Session 3 introduces updates. Session 4 tests that updates are reflected.'
